title: ROTTEN TO THE CORE
text: ROTTEN TO THE CORE by Richard Thomas A man wakes up, remembers what has happened, and screams into the void. # I sit in the back of my bar watching the lights flicker as shadowy figures dance and frolic, all of it lost on me, my cocoon a comforting warmth and denial, the history of my life shoved into the corners of my mind. The booth I’m in is faded leather, torn in places, duct tape holding the puffy innards where they belong, my own guts spilled onto the table, over many a late-night confession. I swirl the amber liquid in a cut glass with heavy weight, sipping at the anesthesia with a singular purpose—escape. These shimmering moons and distant planets orbit my table, interacting, basking in the glow of youth and innocence, unable to see my cracks, the ways my framework is held together with string, and wood, and plaster. It’s a shoddy job, and I don’t expect it to last much longer. It’s one of those dive bars with a clever name, something to help you laugh as you slowly drink yourself to death—The Stumble Inn, The Office, or The Hideout. I bought it when I had nowhere else to go, and nothing else to do. This was my prolonged suicide attempt, since I didn’t have the courage to swallow a bullet the right way. Every now and then a young woman will stumble my way, attracted to the moth in the corner, something about my dark presence appealing to her sense of adventure. The smart ones will come closer, and then take a step back, unsettled by something they see—the glint in my eye, perhaps, the wave of negative energy that surrounds me, maybe, or just the general sense of suffering and emptiness in my manner of dress, messy hair, and uneven beard. Those that are deeper into their drink, less afraid, or seeking something empty to complement their own void, sometimes sit down next to me, chatting me up, putting a hand on my muscled, sweaty forearm, leaning in to get a better look. The last time one went home with me she got up after we were done with our exertions, casting a wary eye my way. She picked up her clothes off the floor and started to get dressed. “I have to go,” she said. “There’s something in here with us, and I don’t like it.” I would have argued with her if she was wrong. “You should get some help, before it eats you alive. Before it finishes the job.” I take a deep breath and exhale. “I was hoping that might be you,” I said. Her fit body glistened in the dim light, as she slowly covered herself back up, putting as many layers of cotton and wool between us as she could. She ran her hands through her long, red hair, untangling it, and then put it up in a ponytail. She couldn’t help glancing at me, looking up as she slid on her socks, peeking over her shoulder as she pulled on her jeans and gray sweatshirt. Keeping an eye on me. Afraid to look away for too long. “This is way beyond me. Get help. And don’t call me.” She crossed to my side of the bed and sat down, trembling, placing a hand on my bare chest. “You’re not a bad man,” she said. “But you have to let this go. It’s toxic. And the waves it gives off are palpable.” She kissed me on the forehead, shuddered, and then left. In the bar, I sit and watch, preferring to stay as far away from the customers as I can. And yet, I hate to be alone. I hate to be alone with my thoughts. To remember. To understand my role in the way things played out. There is no end to the pain that I can see, it goes all the way to my core, and on that bent and swaying framework I hang the last of my withering hope. # In the shadows, there is giggling, a click, and the sound of a heavy door opening, and then tiny footsteps running away. Later there is a loud noise, and even later still, the closing of a door, a bath being run, and a heavy sigh, followed by gentle sobbing. # It’s on a slow night, Wednesday, I think, when the tall, thin man slides into the booth across from me, dressed as if he had an important meeting with a board of directors, or perhaps a body to bury. It’s early still, the long, empty tavern filled with the distant bass and muffled lyrics of some lonely crooner, or goth lizard king. The sparkling of the bottles on the wall echoes the string of sad Christmas lights that run the perimeter of the room, the tang of orange disinfectant barely covering up the sour smells of vomit, urine, and something rotting. He doesn’t say anything at first, only gives me a cursory glance as he sips a martini, his pinkie finger extended. I am currently holding my glass of bourbon, the bottle not quite out of reach, head bent over, gazing into the swirling liquid—always looking for answers. And never finding them. For a moment I wonder if he’s even here, his presence too quiet and still. And then his eyes turn to me, two deep pools of obsidian, and it’s my turn to shiver. His pale skin is almost translucent, blue veins running up his neck, and down the back of his hands. The suit is dark, some shade of black that holds a slight glimmer to it, his dress shirt blindingly white, but no tie. The skin of his neck, it’s a different shade of white, almost a blue tint to it, and as I stare at the indentation above his collar bone, I feel like he might be hairless, like a newly born rat. The way he carries himself as though he is muscled and sinuous, at rest, but ready at a moment’s notice to unleash the violence within. I am both attracted to him, and repulsed at the same time, eager to touch his skin, knowing it must be ice cold, but fearful that it might give way, and reveal something squirming underneath. I shake my head and catch his gaze. “Something I can do for you?” I ask. “Maybe,” he says. He reaches into his suit coat, and pulls out a thin, brown, leather wallet. Though it may not be leather at all. Something about it is off, but I try not to stare. If I didn’t know any better I’d say a few stray hairs stuck out of it, but he opens it and pulls out a business card before I can honestly judge what he carries. He hands the white card to me and I take it. “And maybe I can do something for you in return.” The card says Jeremiah Fisher, Proprietor. There is no name of the business, just an address over on the west side of town, Cicero Avenue. And no phone number or email, just a glyph of three inverted crosses, with several circles dotted around them. “I’ve been watching you for some time,” he says. “I come in for a drink now and then on my way to other destinations. Usually I sit at the other end of the bar. I’ve noticed that you also like to watch the community here pinball around each other, preferring to stay in the shadows, basking in whatever energy they give off.” I shrug my shoulders, and take a sip of my drink. “I’ve also noticed that every now and then one of the inebriated young ladies will approach you, curious as to what presence is giving off such dark vibes from the depths of this seedy bar. No offense intended.” “None taken.” “Usually they move on, and I go back to my drink, relieved. But sometimes they go home with you. And then I never see them again.” I sigh. “I don’t kill them, if that’s what you’re asking.” He grins at me, a mouth full of teeth that is far too wide and full for my taste. “I thought that for a while,” he says, “and then I ran into this boisterous redhead down the street at another establishment, fit as a fiddle. Though every now and then she did seem a bit agitated. She kept looking over her shoulder, toward the door, usually, as if she was expecting someone. And then she went back to her festivities. Bent, but not broken, right?” “I don’t know about that.” “Oh, but you do,” he continued. “And that’s why I’m here. I can tell that you’re suffering. And that you have been for some time. I think I can help you.” I take a deep breath, and exhale. I’ve been here before. Seen therapists, gone to counselors, taken drugs—nothing worked. I know what I did, my role in the tragedies that happened. There are no excuses, and I will never forgive myself. “I have a business proposition for you. Come to my office whenever you have a spare moment. I know that you don’t care about yourself…” he says, picking up the bottle, and filling my glass, “…and I’m not here to stop you from this slow decline into madness and delusion. But you may be able to help others.” He pauses for a moment, his eyes washing over me, and I shiver again, as if a thousand spiders are traversing my flesh, seeking entry. And then he continues. “And that may be the best way to exorcise your demons.” He picks up his drink, extracts the three olives with his other hand, opens his cavernous mouth, and pours the entire martini down his gullet. Grinning, he sets the glass down, and then places the skewer between his teeth, and pulls the olives off, one at a time, chewing slowly, as he closes his eyes in a silent rapture. It unsettles me to no end. “Come by, we’ll talk. I can’t sit in your presence any longer,” he says, standing up, brushing off his suit as if it is covered in dandruff and dog hair. “It’s quite odious, and I feel rather sick.” I open my mouth to say something but he silences me with his hand. “But that’s what I’m looking for. This is who you are.” I close my eyes for a second, and when I open them again, his long, slender legs have skittered across the bar, where he pauses at the door and glances back. He winks, and disappears into the night, his shadow holding the frame for a moment, then departing with him, out into the cold. I tear up the business card and burn it in the fading candle that sits in the center of the table. # When I wake up the next day, at home in my one-bedroom apartment, the darkness and shadows wrap around me like a blanket. There is the sound of a ticking clock, the humming of the refrigerator, and the flush of a toilet from the neighbors above me. Muttering drifts to me, and then several thuds, followed by the scraping of metal, the sound of something cooking in a pan, smoky and meaty, crackling into the air. Outside, something that might be rain, or even snow, shushes and spits, so I burrow under the blankets, eager to hibernate as long as I can. When I turn my head to the nightstand, the business card sits there, intact. Deep inside my empty ribcage there is a long slow moan building, an echo that feels like a scream muffled by rough hands and a thick pillow, my skin suddenly flushed with a slowly building sweat that threatens to spill across my flesh. I throw the card away, and it reappears the next day. I cut it into tiny pieces, and place the bits in three different garbage cans, including the dumpster out back. It’s on the kitchen counter, leaning against the coffee machine, the following day. I drown it in the sink until it separates and dissolves, only to find it in the medicine cabinet soon after. I give up and go to see the man. # In the shadows, there is giggling, a click, and the sound of a heavy metal door opening, and then tiny footsteps running away, fading down the hallway. Later there is a loud noise, a bang, and even later still, the closing of a door, a bath being run, and a heavy sigh, followed by gentle sobbing. # The address is in a nondescript strip mall between a tobacco shop and a questionable massage parlor. There is no sign, no neon, no name—just an address, and a solid door made out what looks like steel, with a heavy wrought iron handle, and rivets running up and down the seams. It is formidable. As I pull it open, I ask myself if it’s here to keep people in, or out? As I walk into the main room, the only space that I can see, there are several chairs placed around the perimeter, as if I’m in the waiting room of some shady doctor, sketchy lawyer, or discount dentist. Plastic chairs, and glass tables with brass lamps, magazines scattered around—it’s as if the entire space was purchased out of a sitcom or some mundane reality show. There is nothing special about this room, the sliding glass windows on the far side hiding whatever secretary or assistant might lurk there. I decided to just show up in the middle of the day, thinking that might be the most logical time to catch them open. There is nobody else here but me, so I walk to the window and rap on the pane of glass. A young woman slides it open, chewing her gum as if there is no tomorrow. She takes a long look at me as I open my mouth to speak, and then holds up her finger to silence me. “Don’t tell me—you met a man, he told you to stop by, and here you are, right?” I nod my head. “Yeah, you reek of it, have a seat, don’t need your name, in fact, please don’t tell it to me, the less I know of you, the better. Go sit ALL the way over there in the corner, and I’ll tell Jerry you’re here.” I start to talk and she waves her manicured nails at me, pulling a pencil out of her prodigious hairdo, squinting her eyes, as she closes the window. She wants nothing to do with me. And I don’t blame her. When I turn around there are three people seated in the room with me, and I take a step back, jostled by their presence. To the left of me is a bedraggled man who is either the second coming of Christ or homeless. His long hair and beard are dotted with motes and flies, a scribbled impurity surrounding him like a cloud of dust. There is a wave of heat coming off him like an oven, and the stench that floats to me is a combination of sour milk, rotting meat, and fermented apples. I gag and cover my mouth. He lowers his gaze from me, picking at the sores on his arms, torn flesh that doesn’t want to heal. “Sorry,” I mutter, and he gently shakes his head. This is when I notice the bandages. On his hands, there are so many dirty white bandages that I’m not sure where his hands and fingers begin, and where they end. He seems to have been in a terrible accident, but the more I take him in, the more I realize it’s something else entirely. He is missing fingers, or parts of fingers, digits, or individual segments. Though he is dressed in ratty jeans, boots, and a dingy blue sweater, I see seeping wounds, divots of flesh, missing parts from head to toe. I turn to head to my seat and hear a gentle cough from my right side. I see a young woman, stylish and slim, dressed as though this was a job interview at a top modeling agency downtown, or perhaps an internship at some prestigious fashion magazine. She is quite literally the opposite of whatever the man is—smiling, pretty, clean, and with a shimmer in her gaze. It’s only when I stare directly at her that I realize that she is translucent. When she moves her hand to fix her hair, which is totally fine, I can see through her flesh, to her veins, and the blood running through them, to the bones, and their movement, one joint connected and moving another piece. When she laughs, averting her eyes, fidgeting in her seat now, she fades a bit more, and sensing this shift, coughs, and covers her mouth. Her smile is gone now, and in her eyes I see fear. “Do you…” I start and she shakes her head. “No, it’s okay, don’t…” she says, and fades a bit more. “But, can I…” “Please,” she says, smiling weakly. “Maybe there is…” And she continues to fade, her smile turns to a frown, and as she opens her mouth she disappears. Gone, in a whisper of smoke. I turn to the man on my left, looking for answers. “She said please,” he growls. “How about you shut up and sit down?” I nod my head, and head to the back. In the other corner is a large Black man. He is one of the biggest men I have ever seen. He is wearing only a black tank top and matching shorts, his feet bare. I wonder if he is a professional wrestler, or perhaps a football player, or bouncer. But his size is not what pulls me in, it’s the tattoos that cover his skin. I’ve never seen white tattoos on a Black man, and they cover him in patterns, symbols, glyphs, hieroglyphics, and words. I am captivated by the tapestry that is his skin, and take a step towards him, to try to take it in. He holds up his hand and motions for me to stop. “This is not for you,” he says, in a deep, rumbling voice. He sneezes, and reaches for a box of tissues, but the red mist that has left his nose is already spraying the space in front of him. He dots his nose, and staunches the bleeding, swallowing as his eyes shift to yellow. I take a step back, already out of the reach of his fluids, but fearful nonetheless. “Sorry,” I say. He nods his head, closes his eyes, and I sneak a glance at his formidable arm, a Bible quote running up and down his meaty limb—”…they shall fall by the sword: their infants shall be dashed in pieces, and their women with child shall be ripped up…” and I step away, unwilling to finish the quote, as his eyes snap open. “I said this isn’t for you,” he bellows, as dots of blood cry down his face. I step towards the corner to take my seat. What have I done in the short span of time I’ve been here? Before I can sit down, the window slides back open. “You, danger boy, enough already, I can’t leave you alone for a second. Just come on back, already. Jesus,” she mutters, sliding it closed, a simple white door to the left popping open. # Jeremiah sits behind a large mahogany desk that looks as though it might have been on a pirate ship, in the captain’s quarters, or perhaps the library of a king. It is carved with intricate moldings and inlays, the legs ending with claws, the heads of various beasts carved into each corner. The walls on three sides of this room are painted a neutral shade of beige, but the shelves behind him are book shelves from the floor to the ceiling, filled to overflowing with ancient texts. Bound in leather, embossed with gold, stamped with metals and glittering stones, these are not the textbooks, novels, and paperbacks you’d find at the corner bookstores. These are tomes, manifestos, and grimoires— The Codex Gigas, The Book of Soyga, The Picatrix al Hikam . The words and titles mean nothing to me, but in the presence of these texts, I feel both uneasy, and at peace. “I’m glad you came,” he says. “I’m sure you have so many questions, but I’m afraid I probably won’t be able to answer them. My clients ask for, and deserve, their privacy. In fact, I apologize for the events outside. I should have known your presence would cause a ripple, as well, but that can’t be avoided, I suppose. You have to come to me when you are ready, and if I only let one patient in at a time, well, we’d be here for all of eternity. No rest for the wicked, right?” I nod my head. “Why am I here, Jeremiah? What is it you do here?” He leans back in his black leather desk chair, and steeples his fingers. “I am a purveyor of goods. I am a healer. I am a priest. I see in others that which they cannot. I see in others those toxins that continue to destroy. When I do my job right, when the moons and planets align, I can create a chemical reaction, an algorithm, a formula that takes one essence from over HERE and moves it over THERE, with very different results. You, my friend, are toxic. You have been anointed with a darkness that has seeped into your bones, quite literally, and it poisons everyone and everything around you.” Jeremiah pauses for a moment, and then gets up to light a series of lavender candles, one in each corner of the room. He then takes a bundle of sage off of a shelf, lights it, blunts it, and then sets it in a large ceramic ashtray in the shape of a skull, pushing it across the desk, away from himself, and toward me. He sits back down and relaxes. “Much better,” he says. “I should have done that before you came in. I have other precautions on hand, but I underestimated your presence, my friend.” He motions around the room to scrolls that hang on the wall, a grouping of crosses, and a woven rug with geometrical patterns. I notice for the first time the rosemary bushes with their purple flowers, green and yellow plants with their sword-like leaves pushing up towards the sky, and what looks like basil growing in a pot with other herbs. “I have a proposal,” he says. “I would like to extract your bone marrow. It is a painful process, not when we extract it, but when the anesthesia wears off. But that’s part of why I’m doing this. Your marrow, if I’m correct, and I usually am, can help others. It can tamp down memories, bad memories, it can help to numb individuals to the pain that they carry with them, and it can allow the body, mind, and soul to heal.” I listen to what he has to say. But I’m not sure if I care. “Why would I do this? What’s in it for me?” Jeremiah nods his head, frowning, as if I’ve let him down. He sighs, and continues. “There will be of course a financial compensation which I think you will find quite adequate. But more importantly, this compound that I manufacture, it can be used for your suffering as well. I can help you to move on. And I think that’s what you want to do. To be able to finally forgive yourself. To heal.” I nod my head. I think of the guy outside, and his discomfort at my reaction, the woman that I spoke to, how she slowly disappeared, and the large man in the corner, with the tattoos that weren’t for me. I decide to do what he says. And in that relief, I sob into my shaking hands. # In the shadows, there is giggling, the laughter of my son, a click, and the sound of a heavy metal door opening, and then tiny footsteps running away, fading down the hallway. Later there is a loud noise, a bang, and even later still, the closing of a door, a bath being run, and a heavy sigh from my distant wife, followed by gentle sobbing. # The procedure works. They extract my marrow, and use it as a catalyst for some compound, which can be turned into a treatment for those that suffer, like myself. I have forgotten that which I needed to forget, and in donating my rotten core to science, am able to heal others. It allows me to rise up out of the darkness and exist in the light again. I sell the bar to some local musicians and they start remodeling right away. I begin dating a nice woman who lives down the street, and I’m able to move on from the trauma of my past. For a while. It takes me some time to see it, but eventually, the pattern emerges. It starts with a walk around the neighborhood, the leaves turning, a cool breeze in the air, as I take in a local park, the laughter and music washing over me in a gentle wave. Relaxing on a bench, a woman approaches me, and sits down. She is muttering to herself, her long red hair peeking out of her dirty gray sweatshirt. Her jeans have holes in the knees, and in her hands she has a bag of popcorn, which she starts to sprinkle on the ground for the pigeons. At first I pay her no attention, the day is filled with the perfume of the season—cut grass, burning leaves, and cinnamon. But underneath it is something earthy, a musty richness, emanating from her, I think, and I’m reminded of the man in the office. When the woman turns to look at me, she smiles. “It’s never that simple,” she says. “What is?” I ask. “Any of it.” She bends over, and sprinkles more popcorn, and the pigeons keep gobbling it up. One of them, a dull grey with a single splash of white on its chest stutters back and forth and tips over. It lays on the ground, twitching, and then stops moving. The woman smiles. The other birds keep eating the popcorn, some seeming to choke on it, others attacking each other, specks of blood blooming on their white feathered chests, a cacophony of screeching and wings fluttering, until in a rush of violence it all goes still, the sidewalk peppered with a dozen dead birds. The woman gets up, her bag empty, fluttering to the ground, a glint in her eye as she leans over and kisses me on the forehead. # I wander home in a daze, past the bar I used to own, which has a CLOSED sign on it, city inspection stickers all over the front door, yellow tape across it. I call the woman I’ve been dating, but a message comes on saying the number has been disconnected, or is no longer in service. I dial it again, but there is nobody there. I pass an alley, and see a familiar shape huddled by a garbage can, the man from the office. All around him is a fog of insects, flies and bees, motes and crickets. As I get closer I see that his body is contorted into an awkward shape, limbs bent, one hand lying in the gravel, each finger severed, and covered up with bandages, that seep red, with yellow rings encrusting each digit. I go back to the office. But it’s empty. I peer inside the dusty glass door, and the chairs are all there, covered in grime. There are dead plants in each corner, the walls covered in graffiti, a smell of stale cigarettes, decaying plants, and mold seeping out of a crack in the glass. I reach into my back jeans pocket looking for the business card, and find it. It says Kinko’s on it, the edges bent, the asterisk of a logo lined with pen marks, turning it into something else entirely Everything comes at a price. And I haven’t paid my fee, not even close. I have taken no responsibility. I was absent when I should have been present. I was weak when I should have been strong. I have not been absolved, because I have not sought redemption, or even asked for forgiveness. In the echo of his bang, in the silence that came after, there was an opportunity to make amends, to change my ways, and to become a better man. Instead, I did nothing. I hid in the shadows. Instead of comforting those that were left, I pushed away, defensive, and denied my guilt. And in the void that I created, the darkness pushed further outward, spiraling into her already weakening framework, pulling her down into the depth with me. And there again, I was the destroyer of worlds, an infection that continued to spread, a disease without a cure. Death is too easy. The punishment must continue. I have not learned a damn thing. So those around me suffer. I pray for guidance now, as I fall back into the darkness. But it will not be coming. Not any time soon. # A man wakes up, remembers what has happened, and screams into the void. When I first conceived of Liminal Spaces , Jessica McHugh was definitely on my list of writers I wanted to involve. After reading her story “Eye of Newt” in Campfire Macabre , I only become more convinced of it. After reading her astounding blackout poetry collection (nominated for a Bram Stoker Award) A Complex Accident of Life, there was no question about it: I had to have a Jessica McHugh story for this collection. She delivered in a big way with her wondrously gruesome and forlorn tale, “Back to One.”
